internalTooling.heroP1: Building the invisible engines that power engineering teams. The work below rarely ships publicly, but it quietly eliminates hours of manual toil every week.
internalTooling.heroP2: These are architectural patterns I design and maintain. Each diagram shows the real technology stack, the data-flow sequence, and the integration points.
internalTooling.iamIntro: Zero-touch onboarding pipeline integrating HR webhooks with Google Workspace and Slack. A new hire is fully provisioned (account, groups, and welcome message) before they walk through the door.
internalTooling.iamOutcome: Reduced average onboarding setup time from ~45 minutes of manual IT work to under 2 minutes of automated execution per new hire.
internalTooling.reportingIntro: Scheduled telemetry aggregation for management visibility. Every morning, teams receive a formatted Slack digest of the previous day's engineering activity without anyone lifting a finger.
internalTooling.reportingOutcome: Replaced a weekly manual report compiled in a spreadsheet with a daily automated digest, increasing visibility cadence 5× with zero additional effort.
internalTooling.philosophyBody: Good internal tooling is invisible. It runs quietly in the background, handles failures gracefully, and never pages anyone at 3 AM. The patterns above share three properties: idempotency (safe to re-run), observability (every step logs to a central system), and fault isolation (a failure in the Slack step never rolls back the GWS provisioning).

iamFlow.label: Trigger
iamFlow.title: HR System Webhook
iamFlow.description: A new-hire event fires an HTTPS webhook the moment HR confirms a start date. The payload carries name, team, role, and required access groups.

iamFlow.label: Processing
iamFlow.title: Python Flask API
iamFlow.description: A lightweight internal service validates the payload schema, looks up the provisioning policy for the role, and orchestrates the downstream calls.

iamFlow.label: Action · Identity
iamFlow.title: Google Workspace Admin API
iamFlow.description: Creates the corporate Google account, assigns it to the correct OU, and adds it to the relevant Google Groups, all in a single atomic sequence.

iamFlow.label: Action · Comms
iamFlow.title: Slack API
iamFlow.description: Sends a personalised Block Kit welcome DM to the new hire and posts a provisioning-complete notification to the IT channel with a summary card.

reportingFlow.label: Trigger
reportingFlow.title: Jenkins Cron Job
reportingFlow.description: A scheduled Jenkins pipeline fires every weekday morning, passing environment parameters (date range, target channels) to the downstream script.

reportingFlow.label: Processing
reportingFlow.title: Python Aggregation Script
reportingFlow.description: Connects to multiple internal databases, aggregates KPIs (deployments, incident count, open PRs), normalises the data, and builds the report payload.

reportingFlow.label: Action
reportingFlow.title: Slack Webhook · Rich Report
reportingFlow.description: Posts a formatted Block Kit message to per-team channels with charts, trend indicators, and direct links to dashboards. Zero manual effort, every day.

underTheHood.milestoneTitle: The i18n refactor: why we moved to dynamic routing
underTheHood.milestoneP1: Although this portfolio started as a single-language experience, the entire app directory was refactored around a dynamic [lang] segment so Spanish and English are first-class routes, not an afterthought. That structural choice keeps layouts, dictionaries, and navigation aligned with how Next.js App Router expects internationalized sites to behave and signals scalability and technical foresight.
underTheHood.milestoneP2: Spanish is the default for most local visitors; English remains available for global reach. Middleware reads Accept-Language, falls back to Spanish when it is a better fit, and redirects bare paths like /tools to /es/tools or /en/tools so bookmarks and shared links always resolve inside a locale.
underTheHood.heroSubtitle: A transparent look at the architecture, infrastructure-as-code, CI/CD pipeline, and engineering process behind this portfolio.
underTheHood.infraP1Mid1: for cost-efficiency. Zero infrastructure to operate and a generous free tier that fits a portfolio workload. However, enterprise roles demand AWS fluency, so the repository includes production-grade
underTheHood.infraP1Mid2: that define the same architecture on AWS: ready to

awsNodes.title: CloudFront
awsNodes.body: Global CDN with TLS termination, WAF integration, and signed-URL support for private content.

awsNodes.title: S3 + OAC
awsNodes.body: Immutable static bundle behind a private bucket. CloudFront accesses it via Origin Access Control, with no public bucket URL.

awsNodes.title: Lambda (Node 20)
awsNodes.body: Handles SSR pages, API routes, and ISR revalidation. Function URL exposes an HTTPS endpoint without API Gateway overhead.

underTheHood.terraformFootnote: The full manifests (main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf) live in /infrastructure/terraform/ in the repository. They define an S3 bucket, a CloudFront distribution with two cache behaviors (static long-TTL vs. SSR passthrough), a Lambda function with a Function URL, and all IAM roles. No state backend is configured to avoid free-tier surprises. A production setup would add an S3 backend with DynamoDB state locking.
underTheHood.cicdIntro: Every push to any branch triggers the GitHub Actions workflow. PRs cannot be merged while any job is red. There is no manual override.

cicdSteps.label: Lint & Type-check
cicdSteps.description: ESLint (strict) + Prettier format check + tsc --noEmit. Catches style drift and type errors before a single byte is compiled.

cicdSteps.label: Build
cicdSteps.description: next build with dummy env vars. Catches missing environment variable references and SSR-incompatible imports at compile time.

cicdSteps.label: Playwright E2E
cicdSteps.description: 30 tests across Chromium + Mobile Safari, run against the production artifact. Tests cover navigation, responsive layout, and page content.

cicdSteps.label: Artifact Upload
cicdSteps.description: The .next/ build output is uploaded as a GitHub artifact (7-day retention) so the E2E job reuses the exact same binary without rebuilding.

underTheHood.specIntroBefore: No code is written without a spec. Every feature starts as a
underTheHood.specIntroAfter: file that defines context, technical decisions, acceptance criteria, and an AI contribution section. The spec is the single source of truth: it is written before the branch is cut and closed when CI is green.

specSteps.text: Identify the feature or fix needed
Build against the spec. AI pair-programming, always reviewed
Open PR using the PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE (includes AI Contribution table)
GitHub Actions: lint → build → Playwright E2E
Squash merge to main with JAG-XXX in the commit message

underTheHood.stackIntro: Every technology choice has a documented reason. No cargo-culting.

techStackRows.why: Server Components, Server Actions, and ISR out of the box
`any` is a lint error; Python handles backend APIs (Flask), Jenkins pipelines, and advanced automation scripts
Utility-first speed with accessible, themeable components
Type-safe queries without the ORM magic overhead
RBAC with zero-infrastructure overhead; JWKS-based JWT verification
Single schema shared between server actions and client forms
Server-side token; client never touches the secret
Streaming text via `streamText` → `toTextStreamResponse()`; Upstash Redis rate-limits the chat route to 5 req/min per IP
Chromium + Mobile Safari; runs against production build in CI
Lint → type-check → build → E2E; PRs blocked on red CI
HCL is cloud-agnostic; demonstrates enterprise AWS readiness
Zero-ops; $0/month on free tier for a portfolio workload

underTheHood.securityIntro: Security is layered. No single control is relied upon exclusively.

securityCards.title: Input validation
securityCards.body: Zod schemas on every Server Action and API route. The same schema validates client-side forms and server-side payloads.

securityCards.title: XSS prevention
securityCards.body: A zero-dependency regex sanitizer strips all HTML tags and null bytes before any string reaches the database.

securityCards.title: Rate limiting
securityCards.body: Upstash Redis sliding-window limiter on all mutating endpoints. The AI Chat route is aggressively capped at 5 requests per minute per IP to prevent abuse and protect billing quotas. Anonymous and authenticated limits are configured separately.

securityCards.title: RBAC (Clerk)
securityCards.body: Anonymous users can only recommend vinyls. The admin (me) can add to collection, update, and delete. Enforced server-side.

securityCards.title: CORS allowlist
securityCards.body: lib/security/cors.ts enforces an explicit origin allowlist. Unknown origins receive a 403 before any handler runs.

securityCards.title: No secrets in code
securityCards.body: All credentials live in .env (gitignored) and GitHub Secrets. .env.example documents the required variables without values.

underTheHood.telemetryP1Mid: page and every Discogs search in the Interactive Lab appends an anonymous row to the
underTheHood.telemetryP1End: table in Neon PostgreSQL. Counts below are live, fresh on every page load.
